Call 2026 - Round 1
Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID: KA152-YOU-557F5044 Deadline (Brussels Time): 12 Feb 2026 12:00:00
Project summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
With “Fair Trade for a Sustainable Tomorrow” we want to create a learning space where young people can critically explore
global trade, reflect on their own role within it and gain the confidence to act in their communities. From a youth work
perspective, the project is not only about increasing knowledge on fair trade, but about strengthening competences,
participation and intercultural dialogue through non-formal education.
Our first objective is to increase young people’s understanding of global supply chains, price distribution and power
relations in international trade. Many young people consume products daily without knowing how they are produced or who
benefits most from their sale. Through experiential methods such as simulations, role plays and debates, we want
participants to move beyond abstract concepts and connect economic systems with human realities. In youth work practice,
this means learning by doing and reflecting, not by listening to lectures. By the end of the exchange, participants should be
able to explain how global trade works in simple terms and identify both the potential and the limits of fair trade.
Our second objective is to develop critical thinking and informed opinion-forming. Topics such as fair trade, ethical
consumption or sustainability are often presented in simplified ways. We want participants to analyse arguments, question
marketing claims and engage with different perspectives respectfully. Through participatory methods like Debate Corners,
Ethical Dilemma Café and Mythbusters Lab, participants practise expressing and defending opinions while listening to
others. This supports youth work goals of empowering young people to become active, critical citizens rather than passive
consumers of information.
Our third objective is to strengthen empathy, intercultural understanding and solidarity. By working in mixed international
teams and exploring case studies from different regions of the world, participants are encouraged to see trade not only as an
economic issue but as a human one. Intercultural evenings, reflection families and creative activities support mutual
understanding between participants from different countries and backgrounds. In youth work practice, this contributes to
breaking stereotypes and building trust across cultures, which is essential for European cooperation.
Our fourth objective is to enhance key competences relevant for personal and civic development. Through non-formal
learning, participants improve communication skills, teamwork, problem-solving and project planning. The Campaign
Design Labs in particular aim to strengthen initiative and entrepreneurship competence, as participants transform ideas into
realistic awareness-raising actions. Reflection sessions connected to Youthpass help them recognise these competences
consciously. This objective links the thematic content with transferable skills that can be used in education, work and
community engagement.
Our fifth objective is to support active participation and follow-up engagement. The project is designed so that participants
not only learn about fair trade, but also create concrete outputs that can be implemented locally. By preparing workshops,
campaigns or small events, they experience that their ideas can have an impact. From a youth work perspective, this is
about moving from awareness to agency. We want participants to feel capable of initiating discussions, sharing knowledge
and taking small but meaningful actions in their communities.
We want to implement this project because we see that many young people feel concerned about global injustice and
sustainability, yet often feel powerless or confused about what they can realistically do. Fair trade is not only an economic
concept, but a gateway to understanding how our everyday choices are connected to global inequalities. By creating a safe,
intercultural and participatory learning space, we want to transform frustration into informed awareness and awareness into
action. Our aim is to help young people realise that they are not just consumers, but citizens with influence. Through shared
reflection, dialogue and practical tools, we want to strengthen their confidence to question systems, make conscious
decisions and initiate change in their own communities.
We aim to combine thematic learning with competence development and intercultural collaboration. Through participatory
non-formal methods, structured reflection and shared responsibility, the project supports young people in becoming more
informed, confident and engaged members of their local and European communities.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The project is a 8-day Erasmus+ Youth Exchange that will bring together 40 young people from Hungary and four partner
countries (Spain, Portugal, Romania and Estonia) as well as 2 facilitators from Hungary, each national group accompanied
by one group leader (min. age of 18). Participants will be between 18 and 30 years old, with an effort to ensure gender
balance within and across national groups. We aim for diversity not only in nationality and gender, but also in educational
background, work experience, social situation and personal motivation. From each country, at least four participants will be
young people with fewer opportunities, including those facing economic, geographical, social or educational barriers.
The profile of participants is intentionally mixed. Some are university students in areas such as economics, social sciences,
environmental studies or international relations. Others are vocational students, young workers, job seekers or young people
who are not currently engaged in formal education. Many are interested in sustainability and social justice topics, but often

Call 2026 - Round 1
Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID: KA152-YOU-557F5044 Deadline (Brussels Time): 12 Feb 2026 12:00:00
have only fragmented knowledge about global trade systems. Some feel frustrated about global inequalities yet do not see
how they can influence them. Others are curious about Erasmus+ but hesitant due to limited English skills, low self-
confidence or lack of international experience. Some fear being judged, not fitting in or not understanding complex economic
concepts. They want a safe space, practical tools and real dialogue instead of abstract theory.
We consciously design the project to move beyond the lower levels of Roger Hart’s Ladder of Participation, where young
people are merely informed or tokenistically involved. Participants are not passive recipients of information. From the
beginning, they contribute ideas, shape discussions and later co-create final outcomes. They take roles in energisers, small
group facilitation, content creation and dissemination planning. This ensures meaningful participation and shared
ownership.
The programme follows a clear learning journey from awareness to action. The first phase focuses on building trust and
connecting everyday life with the topic. Activities such as “Opening & Human Bingo: Where Does It Come From?” and
Supply Chain Mapping help participants realise how global trade is present in daily consumption. Through interactive
exploration rather than lectures, they begin questioning assumptions.
The second phase deepens understanding through experiential methods. The Chocolate Simulation Game and Power Walk:
Global Trade Edition allow participants to experience inequality dynamics in a structured way. Debriefings make explicit
links to real-world economic systems. Debate Corners and Ethical Dilemma Café encourage critical thinking and respectful
disagreement. Participants practise expressing arguments, listening actively and recognising complexity.
Creative sessions such as the Producer Voices Theatre Lab support empathy and intercultural understanding by inviting
participants to represent different perspectives in global trade. Practical workshops like Fair or Fake? Label Investigation
and The Cost of a T-Shirt help them analyse marketing claims and production realities. These sessions combine analytical
skills with emotional engagement.
In the final phase, participants move toward action. During the Campaign Design Labs, they transform learning into concrete
outputs such as workshop plans, PowerPoint presentations, quiz formats, social media mini-campaigns or awareness
concepts that can realistically be implemented at local level. These outputs are presented during the Fair Trade Expo,
reinforcing ownership and confidence.
Daily reflection in small “reflection families” supports self-awareness and links experiences to Youthpass competences.
Intercultural evenings and the city-based activity in Eger strengthen intercultural dialogue and connect global themes with
local context.
If they become part of this mobility, participants gain deeper knowledge about fair trade, improved communication and
teamwork skills, intercultural friendships, increased confidence in English and greater awareness of their role as active
citizens. More importantly, they leave with practical tools and the belief that small actions, informed decisions and shared
dialogue can contribute to more sustainable and fair societies.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
We expect the project to have impact on several interconnected levels: on the participants themselves, on the partner
organisations and on the local communities they belong to. While we remain realistic about our scale as smaller NGOs, we
believe that meaningful change often starts with small but conscious steps.
At participant level, the most immediate result will be increased knowledge and awareness about global trade systems, fair
pricing and the human realities behind everyday products. However, the deeper impact goes beyond knowledge. Through
experiential non-formal methods, participants will develop stronger critical thinking skills, improved communication in an
international environment and greater confidence in expressing their opinions. Many will arrive with doubts about their
English level, their ability to debate complex issues or their role in global systems. By the end of the exchange, we expect
them to feel more capable, more informed and more motivated to act. Reflection processes connected to Youthpass will
help them recognise their development in key competences such as citizenship, entrepreneurship, personal and social
competence and cultural awareness. This self-awareness can positively influence their academic paths, career choices and
civic engagement.
At interpersonal level, the exchange establishes intercultural understanding and solidarity. Working in mixed teams, sharing
cultural evenings and discussing sensitive topics respectfully contributes to breaking stereotypes and building trust.
Participants leave not only with knowledge but with international friendships and a more nuanced understanding of
European diversity. This strengthens European values such as inclusion, respect and cooperation.
The project will also generate tangible results such as our final digital handbook will compile workshop descriptions,
campaign ideas, reflection tools and participant insightsas well as including the outcomes of the Campaign Design Lab,
making the learning accessible beyond the mobility. Short videos, blog articles and social media content will further extend
visibility. These materials are not only documentation but practical tools that can be reused in schools, youth clubs or future
projects. The Campaign Design Lab outputs, such as ready-to-use presentations and quizzes, enable participants to
organise follow-up activities in their communities.
At local level, we expect ripple effects through dissemination. Each national group will organise at least one local sharing
event, reaching peers who did not participate in the exchange. These events connect global trade issues to local realities
and introduce Erasmus+ opportunities to new audiences. Even small-scale workshops or presentations can inspire further
engagement and encourage other young people to participate in international mobility.
For partner organisations, the project strengthens international cooperation and professional development. By exchanging
methods and co-facilitating sessions, youth workers improve their competences in non-formal education and inclusion. The
shared planning, implementation and evaluation processes contribute to organisational learning. The partnership may also

lead to future collaborative initiatives, reinforcing long-term European cooperation.
On a broader societal level, the impact may be modest but meaningful. When young people become more conscious
consumers, more critical thinkers and more active citizens, this influences conversations in their families, schools and
communities. Even small changes in purchasing habits or public discussions contribute to raising awareness about fairness
and sustainability.
We expect the project to result in informed, confident and connected young people, practical educational materials that
remain accessible, strengthened partnerships between grassroots organisations and a multiplier effect through local
dissemination. The impact lies not only in what is produced, but in the attitudes, competences and relationships developed
during and after the exchange.

Please briefly present your organisation/the group.
The Street of Youth Association is a youth organization based in Budapest, Hungary, and composed of Generation Z
members, specifically focusing on the challenges, problems, and issues faced by Generation Z youth (18-25 years old
youngsters). Our specialty is that we achieve this primarily by focusing on international projects and non-formal education.
We believe in the power of non-formal education and in the power of Erasmus+. We understand that Gen Z young people
are greatly helped through these project, especially with starting their careers, finding their path, and developing their soft
skills.
Our members are exclusively Generation Z youth who want to contribute to their own community and support other young
people. In the association, both the management and the local programs, as well as the international projects, are carried
out by young people, for young people. We believe this is the most effective way to truly make an impact on the lives of other
young people, engage with them and have a positive influence on our environment.
We can identify with the fact that this generation has completely different needs and is driven by different topics compared
to previous generations.
Accordingly, there are three topics that we prioritize and consider the most important in our organization: the mental health

of Gen Z young people; environment protection and sustainability; and the challenges related to the digital world, particularly
social media.
We also believe in youth empowerment. It is important to mention that our association operates on a similar principle to a

What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
Street of Youth Association is a youth-led organiza1on committed to providing high-quality non-formal learning opportunities
for young people. Established in February 2023, the association is composed of university students and young professionals
with significant experience in Erasmus+ youth exchanges, training courses, and youth participation projects. The
organization works to address key challenges faced by young people today while promoting international mobility and
meaningful learning experiences.
One of the main focus areas is mental health and well-being, particularly in relation to work-life balance, career-related
stress, and excessive online presence. To address these concerns, the association is currently developing a podcast series,
which is led by young people and features expert insights. This initiative aims to raise awareness and provide practical
solutions for young people struggling with digital challenges and maintaining a healthy work-life balance.
Environmental protection and sustainability are also central themes in the association’s activities. In addition to preparing an
Erasmus+ Youth Exchange on sustainable habits planned for 2025, several sustainability-focused workshops and actions
have already been implemented. A session in a zero-waste coffee shop introduced participants to practical ways of reducing
daily waste and adopting more environmentally responsible consumption habits. Another workshop focused on clothes
sharing and sustainable fashion, encouraging young people to explore ethical alternatives to fast fashion. A plogging activity
on Margaret Island combined physical exercise with environmental responsibility, highlighting the importance of collective
action in maintaining clean public spaces. A separate workshop on sustainable tourism allowed participants to reflect on
how to minimize their environmental footprint while traveling.
Digitalization and its effects on young people are another key concern for the association. The focus is on promoting
conscious social media use, developing critical thinking skills, and encouraging screen time management. In a world where
young people are constantly connected, the association seeks to foster mindful media consumption and digital well-being.
Workshops and local events provide opportunities for young people to analyze their digital habits and develop healthier
approaches to using technology.
In addition to international projects, the association emphasizes local activities that allow members and other young people
to connect offline. Regular events such as hiking trips, soccer games, and board game nights create a welcoming
environment for social interaction, community building, and meaningful discussions.
The association is also dedicated to promoting Erasmus+, Visegrad, and other short-term mobility programs to ensure that
more young people are aware of these opportuni1es. Many young people have limited access to information about
international programs, and the association aims to make these opportunities more accessible to a broader audience. By
using social media strategically, the organization shares valuable resources, testimonials, and application guidance to
engage and inform young people in an approachable way.

Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Established as an official NGO in 2023, Colony of Creators began its journey as a group of dedicated young individuals in
2017. Through persistence and perseverance, our mission is to expand and offer more opportunities to the youth.
Colony of Creators is a non-profit, non-governmental organization that advances knowledge, entrepreneurship, civic society
development, non-formal education, and innovative learning techniques, primarily targeting the youth (15 to 35). We uphold
values of tolerance, democracy, diversity, voluntariness, and openness.
Our objective is to stimulate local community development via collaborations across private, public, and civil society sectors.
We advocate for inclusivity in non-formal education and foster a spirit of innovation and entrepreneurship. We are driven by
the determination to create a meaningful impact and provide an expanded array of opportunities for young people.
In addition to serving people with disabilities, our broad scope of operations covers areas such as human rights, anti-
trafficking, reproductive health, gender equality, sports, media production, event organization, technology, humanitarian
actions, criminality and corruption prevention, start-ups, project management, refugee support, and publishing.
Our key goals are safeguarding the environment, fostering youth engagement at a global scale, and creating a conducive
work environment. We undertake initiatives such as conducting research on gamified activities, disseminating information,
collaborating with international associations, and encouraging collaborations for environmental protection.
Our focus areas include:
Youth Empowerment: Prioritizing equal opportunities, supporting potential realization, and including youth issues in policies.
Education and Training: Providing affordable, quality, and skill-oriented education with guidance in soft skills and gender-
violence prevention.
Youth and Non-Formal Education: Improving information access, enhancing media sector employment, combating fake

What are the activities and experience of the partner organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your
organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
Formed by a gorup of friends that met through a youth mobility program, our organization aims to educate young people
about environmental consciousness and community involvement. We conduct various activities like lectures, workshops,
and training sessions, centered around themes like urbanization, immigration, social policies, and environmental challenges,
while advocating for democratic participation at all levels.
As first-time applicants to the Erasmus Plus Youth Workers Mobility Programme, we plan to broaden our intercultural
studies by interacting with individuals from diverse cultures. Our experienced team, with a decade of involvement in
international mobilization projects and participation in the Erasmus Plus program across various countries, adds to our
competency.
Our approach emphasizes peacebuilding, inclusion, and tolerance, tackling issues like minority rights, LGBTIQ+ concerns,
and refugee support. We value non-formal education and strive to provide young people access to such resources. Our past
engagements with the Erasmus+ Youth in Action program involve numerous youth exchanges and training courses.
Comprising dynamic members spread across Portugal, our organization celebrates diversity in ideas and experiences. Our
robust engagement with nearly 2000 social media followers, over 200 national and international partners, along with multiple
schools, organizations, and sports clubs, highlights our expansive network. Recognized for our innovative solutions and
result-oriented approach, we remain dedicated to making a positive impact on as many individuals and communities as
possible.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
ANDREIA TEIXEIRA (Director & Senior Project Manager) Andreia initiated her journey in youth projects back in 2013, and
since then, she has gained broad experience in various areas, from organizing social events to managing large groups and
delivering training. She began volunteering within her community at the age of 18, demonstrating her early commitment to
social service. In 2014, Andreia participated in an EVS focusing on assisting disadvantaged children and minority
communities, employing non-formal education methods with a particular emphasis on theater and art inclusion. Post this
engagement, she continued contributing to various projects, both as a participant and a trainer. Professionally, Andreia has
a keen interest in creative projects, sports, human rights, gender equality, and inclusion. She has served as a trainer in over
20 countries, coordinating more than 80 mobility experiences under the Erasmus Plus program in collaboration with various
organizations. Presently, as the director of Colony of Creators NGO, Andreia spearheads international projects and
collaborations while managing international volunteers. She is also an International teacher course provider, focusing on
areas like non-formal education, art for inclusion, outdoor sports, bullying, ICT, and project management. In addition to her
main role, Andreia is a part of a consortium of organizations from Portugal, Romania, Sweden, Spain, and Italy, where these
NGOs offer Teacher courses for schools under the Erasmus Plus program. The subjects covered include non-formal
education, ICT tools for the classroom, Art for inclusion, Sustainability, and outdoor activities. Andreia's passion for
creativity shines through her academic journey. With travel experience to over 60 countries, some facilitated by YIA and
Erasmus+, she has developed a deep understanding of various global perspectives. As a trainer, her specializations include
non-formal education, art for inclusion, outdoor sports, bullying, ICT, project management, entrepreneurship, sustainability,
digital skills, media literacy, and sports.

Project rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
We want to carry out “Fair Trade for a Sustainable Tomorrow” because we see a clear gap between young people’s
everyday consumption and their understanding of how global trade systems actually work. During our previous youth
exchanges and local workshops, we repeatedly experienced that participants care about sustainability, climate change and
social justice, but when the conversation turns to trade, pricing or supply chains, the topic feels distant and abstract. At the
same time, they drink coffee, eat chocolate and wear fast fashion every day without knowing who stands behind these
products. This contradiction is one of the starting points of our project.
The idea of the exchange was inspired by David Ransom’s book The No-Nonsense Guide to Fair Trade, which presents
Fair Trade not as a simple moral solution, but as a complex response to structural inequalities in global markets. We found
this approach very relevant. In discussions with our members and partner organisations, it became clear that young people
rarely get the space to critically examine how global trade works, who holds power in supply chains and what role they
themselves can realistically play. Economic education in schools often focuses on theory, not on lived realities of producers
in countries such as Mexico or Kenya, which we explore during the project.
The issues we want to address are concrete. According to the International Labour Organization, hundreds of millions of
workers worldwide are still employed in vulnerable conditions, many of them in agriculture and textile production. The World
Bank highlights that smallholder farmers often receive only a tiny fraction of the final retail price of products sold in Europe.
At the same time, the European Commission reports that young people are increasingly concerned about sustainability, yet
many feel that their individual actions have limited impact. This sense of powerlessness is something we have observed
ourselves in reflection sessions during previous mobilities.
Another issue is the oversimplified narrative around ethical consumption. Fair Trade is sometimes perceived either as a
perfect solution or as pure marketing. Through personal conversations with participants, we noticed that many young people
either do not trust certification systems or cannot afford Fair Trade products regularly. There is a need for honest discussion
that acknowledges both the strengths and the limitations of Fair Trade, instead of promoting black-and-white thinking.
This project responds to these needs in a practical way. The timetable reflects a clear learning path. We begin with
understanding global interdependence through activities such as Human Bingo and Supply Chain Mapping. We then
explore inequality through the Chocolate Simulation Game and the Power Walk, allowing participants to experience
structural imbalances. With sessions like “Producer Voices Theatre Lab” and “From Mexico to Kenya,” we connect
economic concepts to human stories. Later, in the Mythbusters Lab and Ethical Dilemma Café, participants critically analyse
arguments and confront their own assumptions. Finally, in the Campaign Design Labs and the Fair Trade Expo, they
transform knowledge into concrete, realistic actions that they can implement at home.
We want to carry out this project because we believe young people need tools, not only awareness. They need the ability to
analyse information, question marketing claims, discuss ethical dilemmas and design small but meaningful initiatives in their
communities. As a small youth organisation, we do not aim to change the global trade system in one week. What we can
realistically do is strengthen critical thinking, empathy and civic engagement among 40 young people who can then multiply
the impact.
Our objectives are therefore clear and limited, so that we can genuinely fulfil them:
To increase participants’ understanding of global supply chains, price distribution and power relations in international trade.
To develop critical thinking regarding Fair Trade, including its benefits, limitations and common myths.
To strengthen empathy by exploring the perspectives of producers and workers in different regions.
To empower participants to design and implement at least one realistic awareness-raising activity in their local communities
after the exchange.
To support participants in recognising their own learning process through structured reflection and Youthpass.
These objectives are directly supported by the programme structure: experiential simulations build understanding, debates
and dilemma cafés develop analysis skills, theatre and storytelling build empathy and the Campaign Design Labs ensure
concrete follow-up.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
Our project is closely linked to the objectives of the Erasmus+ Programme and specifically to the aims of Youth Exchanges.
At its core, the project supports active citizenship, participation in democratic life, intercultural dialogue and the
development of key competences through non-formal education.
The topic of fair trade is directly connected to EU values such as human dignity, equality, solidarity and respect for human
rights. The European Union promotes sustainable development, responsible consumption and global responsibility both
internally and in its external action. Trade policies, ethical production and sustainability are not distant political concepts;
they shape everyday life in Europe. By helping young people understand global supply chains, power relations and labour

conditions, we are encouraging them to reflect on how European consumers are connected to global inequalities and how
EU citizens can act responsibly. This strengthens their sense of belonging to a wider European and global community.
The project also reflects the priorities of Erasmus+ in the youth field. One of the key objectives of Youth Exchanges is to
promote intercultural learning and mutual understanding. Throughout the programme, participants work in international
groups, share perspectives from their different national contexts and learn from each other. Sessions such as the Supply
Chain Mapping, the Chocolate Simulation Game and the Ethical Dilemma Café rely on mixed teams where participants
bring their own realities into the discussion. The two Culture Nights are not simple presentations but spaces for dialogue,
curiosity and informal learning. Participants discover similarities and differences in lifestyles, economic realities and
consumer habits. This helps break stereotypes and builds respect across cultures.
Another central element of Erasmus+ Youth Exchanges is learning through non-formal education. Our methodology is
entirely based on participatory, experiential learning. Instead of lectures, we use simulations, role plays, debates, theatre,
world café discussions and creative workshops. Participants are not passive listeners but active contributors. For example,
in the Producer Voices Theatre Lab, they step into the roles of farmers and workers; in the Mythbusters Lab, they critically
analyse common assumptions; in the Campaign Design Labs, they transform knowledge into concrete action plans. This
approach reflects the Erasmus+ understanding that learning happens best when young people are actively involved and
when reflection is integrated into the process.
Reflection is a strong component of our project and is fully aligned with the Youthpass process. Each day ends with
structured reflection time in small “reflection families”, where participants process their experiences, identify learning
moments and connect them to personal development. The Youthpass is introduced early in the programme, not just as a
certificate, but as a tool for recognising competences such as critical thinking, communication, teamwork, cultural
awareness and civic engagement. The final reflection and Youthpass ceremony make learning visible and support
participants in articulating their development. This contributes to one of the key objectives of Erasmus+: recognising and
validating non-formal learning.
The project also strengthens participation and youth initiative. In the Campaign Design Labs and the Fair Trade Expo,
participants use the knowledge gained during the exchange to prepare realistic, ready-to-implement awareness-raising
activities. This reflects the Youth Exchange objective of empowering young people to become active in their communities.
Rather than staying at the level of discussion, the project encourages follow-up actions and dissemination in local contexts.
Participants plan concrete steps for implementation and consider how to measure impact, which supports long-term
engagement.
Furthermore, the project supports inclusion and equal participation. By working in small, stable reflection groups and mixed
international teams, we ensure that every participant has space to speak and contribute. Non-formal methods allow young
people with different educational backgrounds and language levels to participate meaningfully. The programme combines
structured sessions with informal spaces such as board game evenings, movie night and free time, recognising that
intercultural learning also happens outside formal workshops.
The project contributes to building a stronger European dimension in youth work. Participants do not only discuss fair trade
as a global issue; they examine how it appears in their own countries and how EU consumers are part of global systems.
They learn from and with each other, comparing realities from different Member States and partner countries. This shared
exploration strengthens European solidarity and encourages young people to see themselves as active European citizens.
Impact
How will your project benefit the young participants involved in the project, during and after the project lifetime?
The youth exchange is designed to place young people at the centre of the learning process, not as passive recipients but
as active contributors. We are strongly inspired by Roger Hart’s Ladder of Participation and aim to operate clearly above the
lower rungs of non-participation. Participants are not simply informed or consulted; they co-create discussions, shape
outputs and take responsibility for follow-up actions. This approach ensures that the benefits are meaningful both during and
after the project.
During the exchange, participants benefit first of all from a structured non-formal learning environment. Through simulations
such as the Chocolate Game and the Power Walk, they experience global inequalities in a tangible way. In debates, theatre
exercises and dilemma cafés, they learn to express opinions, listen actively and defend arguments respectfully. These
activities strengthen communication skills, critical thinking and social competence. They also develop empathy by stepping
into the roles of producers and workers in global supply chains, which challenges stereotypes and broadens perspectives.
The project addresses real-world problems: unequal distribution of value in global trade, lack of transparency in supply
chains, misleading marketing practices and the feeling of powerlessness many young people experience regarding global
issues. Instead of presenting simple answers, we offer space for honest exploration. Participants learn about Fair Trade
principles, certification systems and their limitations. They analyse case studies and compare realities across countries. This
deep engagement helps them move from superficial awareness to informed understanding.
Throughout the week, participants develop competences aligned with the eight key competences for lifelong learning. They
strengthen literacy and multilingual skills through discussions and presentations in an international environment. They
enhance digital competence when planning awareness campaigns and considering online dissemination. Personal, social
and learning-to-learn competences are supported through daily reflection sessions and the Youthpass process. Civic
competence grows as they explore global responsibility and democratic dialogue. Cultural awareness and expression are
supported during intercultural evenings and mixed group work. Entrepreneurial competence is developed during the
Campaign Design Labs, where participants turn ideas into realistic action plans.

What will the participants learn about the chosen topic of the activity? Which learning outcomes or competences (i.e.
knowledge, skills and attitudes/behaviours) are to be acquired/improved by participants in the activity?
Participants will gain a deeper and more realistic understanding of how global trade systems work and how they are
personally connected to them. The learning is not limited to knowing what Fair Trade is, but to understanding the wider
context: supply chains, price distribution, power relations, certification systems, consumer responsibility and the limits of
individual action.
At knowledge level, participants will learn how products such as coffee, chocolate or cotton travel from producers to
European markets. They will understand how value is distributed along the chain and why small farmers often receive only a
small percentage of the final price. They will explore the principles behind Fair Trade, including minimum price, social
premium and labour standards and also discuss criticisms and limitations. Through case studies such as “From Mexico to
Kenya” and activities like the Chocolate Simulation Game, they will connect economic concepts to real human experiences.
At skills level, the project focuses strongly on critical thinking and analysis. Through debates, myth-busting sessions and
ethical dilemma cafés, participants practise evaluating arguments, questioning marketing claims and forming balanced
opinions. In role plays and theatre exercises, they develop empathy and perspective-taking. In the Campaign Design Labs,
they improve project planning, teamwork, communication and creativity. They learn how to transform knowledge into
concrete, realistic awareness-raising actions.
At attitude level, we aim to strengthen a sense of responsibility, solidarity and openness. Participants are encouraged to
reflect on their own consumption habits without guilt or pressure, but with awareness. By meeting peers from different
countries and discussing how Fair Trade appears in their national contexts, they develop respect for diverse perspectives
and a stronger sense of European and global interconnectedness.
The project supports the development of all eight key competences for lifelong learning, which are reflected in the
Youthpass process:
E.g.:
1. Literacy competence is developed through reading short case materials, discussing complex topics and presenting ideas
clearly in group work and final outputs.
2. Multilingual competence is strengthened as participants communicate in English throughout the exchange. They practise
expressing opinions, negotiating and presenting in an international setting.
3. Mathematical competence and competence in science, technology and engineering are addressed when participants
analyse price distribution, percentages in supply chains and environmental impacts connected to production and transport.
4. Digital competence is supported during campaign planning, where participants design social media content, think about
online communication and critically assess digital information related to Fair Trade.
5. Personal, social and learning to learn competence is central to the project. Through daily reflection sessions and
Youthpass self-evaluation, participants become more aware of their strengths, learning styles and areas for improvement.
6. Citizenship competence is directly linked to the topic. Participants explore their role as active citizens within the EU and
globally, discussing democratic participation, ethical consumption and social justice.
7. Entrepreneurship competence is developed in the Campaign Design Labs, where participants create feasible awareness
actions, define target groups, allocate tasks and plan implementation steps.
8. Cultural awareness and expression competence is strengthened through intercultural exchange, Culture Nights, Treasure
hunt and discussions on how trade and consumption differ across countries. Participants learn to appreciate diversity and
express their own cultural background confidently.

Overall, participants leave the project with a clearer understanding of global trade, stronger analytical and communication
skills and a more responsible and reflective attitude towards consumption and civic engagement. The learning outcomes are
realistic but meaningful: increased awareness, improved competences and concrete ideas they can implement in their
communities.
What are the basic elements of the activity? Please describe at the very least the venue(s), non formal learning methods
used, aims of the session etc.
The activity is a 8-day youth exchange implemented in Hungary, in the city of Eger. The venue is a youth-friendly
accommodation with seminar rooms large enough for plenary sessions (40+ people), as well as smaller break-out spaces
for group work. The location was chosen intentionally: Eger is accessible by public transport, reducing the need for
individual car travel and it offers both indoor facilities and safe outdoor spaces. This allows us to combine structured
workshop sessions with outdoor reflection, city-based activities and informal intercultural moments. The accommodation
provides basic technical support such as projector, flipchart, stable Wi-Fi and common areas, which are sufficient for our
non-formal educational approach without requiring complex infrastructure.
The timetable includes a full learning flow from introduction to follow-up planning. While all sessions are interconnected,
several key elements illustrate the core of the activity.
The programme begins with group-building and introduction to the project. Thematic activities such as “Opening & Human
Bingo – Where Does It Come From?” use interactive icebreakers to connect everyday products to global supply chains. The
aim is to create a safe and open learning environment while immediately linking the topic to participants’ real lives. Non-
formal methods such as movement-based games, small group discussions and visual mapping are used to lower barriers
and encourage participation.
To explore global inequalities, we use experiential simulations. In “The Chocolate Simulation Game”, participants take on
roles within a supply chain (farmers, traders, brands, retailers) and negotiate prices. The aim is to make power imbalances
visible and tangible. In the “Power Walk – Global Trade Edition”, participants physically experience inequality by stepping
forward or staying behind based on role descriptions. These methods are based on experiential learning: instead of
explaining injustice theoretically, participants feel and analyse it through structured debriefing.
Critical thinking is developed through dialogue-based methods. “Debate Corners” and “Ethical Dilemma Café” encourage
participants to position themselves, listen to opposing arguments and reflect on complex issues such as affordability versus
fairness. The aim is not to impose opinions but to develop analytical skills and respectful communication.
Creative expression is another important element. In the “Producer Voices Theatre Lab”, small groups create short scenes
representing farmers or workers in global supply chains. This method strengthens empathy and cultural awareness.
Similarly, “Fair or Fake? Label Investigation” uses problem-solving and group analysis to examine product labels and
marketing claims. The aim is to equip participants with practical tools to question information critically.
Action-oriented learning is central to the final phase of the programme. In the two “Campaign Design Lab” sessions,
participants use the knowledge gained to create concrete awareness-raising outputs: workshop outlines, social media mini-
campaigns, quiz materials or small event concepts. These are realistic and ready to use. The aim is to transform learning
into civic engagement and ensure sustainability beyond the exchange.
Daily reflection is integrated through small “reflection families”. Methods include guided questions, visual mapping,
positioning exercises and personal storytelling. This supports learning-to-learn competence and connects directly to the
Youthpass process. The final Youthpass ceremony highlights competence development and self-evaluation.
Intercultural learning is embedded throughout the activity. Culture Nights are fully prepared by participants, creating space
for sharing traditions, food, music and perspectives. Informal activities such as board game evenings or a movie night
connected to the theme support relationship-building and peer learning outside structured sessions.
The choice of venue supports these methods. The combination of indoor seminar rooms and accessible outdoor areas
allows us to implement simulations, movement-based activities and reflective walks. The city-based treasure hunt connects
the global topic to local context, showing how sustainability and consumption are relevant in everyday life. The venue’s
accessibility by train and bus encourages environmentally conscious travel, aligning with the sustainability theme of the
project.
The basic elements of the activity are experiential simulations, dialogue-based debates, creative expression, action planning
and structured reflection. The aim is to combine knowledge about fair trade with skill development and attitude change in a
participatory, non-formal learning environment that is realistic and feasible for small NGOs.

Cooperation and communication between the participant groups will be structured, simple and transparent. As small NGOs,

selected participants.

Project design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
Preparation and support are central elements of this project. As small youth organisations, we work closely with participants
before, during and after the exchange to ensure they feel informed, safe and actively involved at every stage.
Before the activity
Preparation starts already during the planning phase. The topic and structure of the programme were discussed with young
people from our local group and partner organisations. We organised informal meetings to collect their ideas and
expectations, hence these discussions influenced the content of the sessions. Our partners are organisations we already
know or have worked with and they are motivated to participate and contribute actively. We agree in advance which
organisation will take responsibility for certain sessions, for example sharing a method they are experienced in, leading
energisers or supporting reflection groups.
Selection is transparent and based on an open call shared on social media and through local youth networks. Interested
young people fill in an application form where they describe their motivation, previous experience and availability. We look
for diversity in background and ensure inclusion of young people with fewer opportunities. During selection, we also ask
about dietary needs, medical conditions or other special requirements so that we can prepare accordingly. Personal data is
handled confidentially.
After selection, participants receive a detailed infopack by email, including information about the venue, travel
arrangements, insurance, programme overview, house rules, what to bring and contact details. Each national group has at
least one preparatory meeting led by the group leader. In this meeting, we discuss expectations, responsibilities,
intercultural awareness and practical matters. Participants are encouraged to ask questions openly.
We also organise at least one international online meeting before the exchange so participants can meet each other and
reduce anxiety. During this meeting, we introduce the theme, clarify ground rules and explain the learning approach.
Participants receive small “homework” tasks, such as researching how Fair Trade appears in their country or preparing
something interactive for Culture Night. This creates early engagement and connection.
Regarding linguistic preparation, English is the working language. We ask for a basic communicative level, but fluency is not
required. Participants with stronger English skills are encouraged to support others. Group leaders also assist in translation
or clarification if needed. The atmosphere is supportive rather than competitive.
Risk prevention is addressed clearly. Participants receive information about local emergency contacts, travel safety and
behavioural expectations. We emphasise respect, anti-discrimination and zero tolerance for harassment. Mental well-being
is also considered; participants are informed that they can approach their group leader or hosting team as well as the
facilitators at any time.
During the activity
Support continues throughout the exchange. Group leaders play an important role in monitoring well-being and checking in
daily with their national group. The hosting team holds short coordination meetings with group leaders to address any issues
quickly.
Health and dietary needs are respected. The accommodation is informed in advance about vegetarian, vegan, allergy-
related or religious dietary requirements. Participants who require medication are reminded to bring it and inform leaders if
support is needed.
Intercultural preparation becomes practice during the exchange. Through icebreakers, reflection groups and culture nights,
participants explore differences and similarities in a structured way. Clear ground rules are co-created on the first day,
promoting mutual respect and open communication.
Reflection families provide daily space to process experiences emotionally and intellectually. This helps prevent
misunderstandings and supports personal development. Youthpass is introduced at the beginning and revisited regularly,
encouraging self-evaluation and awareness of learning.
After the activity
After returning home, support continues through follow-up communication. Participants remain connected via common
online groups. We encourage them to share local dissemination activities and provide guidance when they want to organise
workshops or campaigns.
Each participant receives their Youthpass certificate and we offer short follow-up conversations (online if needed) to help
them reflect on how to use it for studies or employment. Those interested in further involvement can join their local
organisation’s activities or contribute to future Erasmus+ ideas.
In all phases, young people are not only prepared and supported but actively involved. They co-shape the programme, take
responsibility during implementation and are encouraged to continue engagement afterwards. This continuous support
structure ensures safety and inclusion.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
The safety and protection of participants is a priority throughout the entire project. As a youth-led organisation, we are
realistic about our size and resources, but we approach safety in a structured and multi-layered way. Our risk assessment
plan covers physical safety, mental and emotional well-being, and the educational/youth work dimension of the activity.
1. Physical safety and practical arrangements

The exchange takes place in Eger, in an accommodation that is easily accessible by public transport and located in a safe
area. The venue offers appropriate capacity for 40+ participants, meeting rooms, common areas and clear emergency exits.
Before choosing the venue, we have checked basic safety standards such as fire regulations, emergency contacts and
accessibility.
Participants receive an infopack before arrival including the full address, emergency numbers, transport guidance and
contact details of the hosting team and the facilitators. All participants are required to have valid travel and health insurance.
This is clearly communicated in advance and confirmed before travel.
A local organiser or facilitator is always present at the venue during the programme. When participants leave the venue for
organised outdoor activities (e.g. city-based treasure hunt), at least one local team member accompanies the group. For free
time, participants are advised to move in small groups and are informed about safe areas and basic local rules.
Each participant signs a participation pledge before or at the beginning of the exchange. This includes:
- Respect for others and zero tolerance for discrimination or harassment
- No use of drugs or illegal substances
- Responsible alcohol consumption in line with local law
- Full participation in programme activities
- Responsibility for valid documents and insurance
- Respect for accommodation rules and hygiene standards
We clearly communicate accommodation-related safety rules: quiet hours, no strangers in rooms, respect for shared spaces
and hygiene standards. Hygiene guidance (basic handwashing, ventilation of rooms, food safety awareness) is shared
especially in shared living conditions.
Participants are reminded to bring their own necessary medication. We explicitly explain that medicines available in Hungary
may differ from those in their home countries, so they should bring personal medicines for headaches, allergies or chronic
conditions. If someone feels unwell, group leaders and organisers support them in contacting local medical services if
needed.
2. Mental and emotional well-being
Safety is not only physical. The programme includes daily reflection groups where participants can express concerns or
discomfort in a smaller, trusted setting. Group leaders and facilitators are trained to observe group dynamics and to identify
early signs of conflict or emotional stress.
Clear ground rules are co-created on the first day to ensure a respectful and inclusive environment. We apply a zero-
tolerance policy towards bullying, harassment or discriminatory behaviour. Participants are informed about who they can
approach in case of conflict: their group leader, a facilitator or both.
The programme balances intensive thematic sessions with informal and lighter activities to avoid overload. Free time and
informal evenings are included intentionally to support well-being.
3. Educational and youth work safety
As a non-formal educational project, we also consider learning safety. Activities such as simulations or debates can trigger
strong emotions. Facilitators ensure proper debriefing after exercises like the Power Walk or Chocolate Simulation.
Participants are never forced to share personal experiences beyond their comfort zone.
Participation is encouraged but not coercive. If someone needs a short break from an activity for personal reasons, this is
respected and discussed privately.
4. Data protection and GDPR
We collect only necessary personal data (name, contact details, dietary needs, health-related information relevant to safety).
This data is stored securely and only for the duration of the project and reporting period, in line with GDPR regulations.
Photos and videos are used only with prior written consent.
5. Ongoing monitoring and coordination
Throughout the exchange, daily coordination meetings between group leaders and the hosting team allow us to identify and
address any emerging risks quickly. This continuous monitoring ensures that small issues do not escalate.
What activities are foreseen after the end of the Youth Exchange? How will the participants follow-up on the activity?
The end of the Youth Exchange is not the end of the process. Follow-up is planned as a realistic continuation of the learning
and outcomes developed during the mobility. As small NGOs, we focus on actions that are feasible, youth-led and
adaptable to local realities.
Immediately after returning home, each national group will organise at least one local sharing activity. This can take the form
of a small workshop in a school or youth club, a presentation during a local NGO meeting or an informal discussion event.
The ready-to-use materials created during the Campaign Design Labs (workshop outlines, quiz questions, social media
mini-campaign drafts, poster concepts) will be used directly in these dissemination actions. Participants will take the lead in
facilitating these activities, supported by their group leaders if needed.
Social media dissemination is an important and realistic tool for us. Within two weeks after the exchange, participants will
prepare short posts, photos or short videos summarising their learning experience. These will be shared on the social media
platforms of each partner organisation and, where possible, on participants’ personal accounts. The hosting organisation
will coordinate a common visual identity and shared hashtags to ensure visibility. Erasmus+ visibility rules will be respected
in all posts.
Each partner organisation will also publish at least one longer post on their website or blog describing the project, its
objectives and outcomes. Participants will be invited to write short reflection texts about what they learned and how they
plan to apply it. This gives them ownership of the dissemination process. Where possible, short testimonial videos will be

At local level, the follow-up activities aim to reach other young people who did not participate in the mobility. Through

consumption. This may also include cooperation with local cafés, small shops or youth centres interested in sustainability

At regional and national level, partner NGOs will share the results within their existing youth networks. This may include

are relevant national youth or sustainability-related events taking place within a reasonable timeframe after the exchange
and if participation is feasible, we will explore presenting the project results there. Any larger external event involvement will
only be confirmed if realistically manageable and aligned with the topic.
At international level, the partnership will stay connected through existing communication channels (WhatsApp group, email,
shared Google Drive). Participants will be encouraged to share updates about their local follow-up actions. The prepared
materials can also be shared on European youth platforms or within Erasmus+ related networks, so that other organisations
can adapt and use them.
We also see follow-up in terms of continued engagement. Participants who are motivated may become more active in their
local NGOs, support future Erasmus+ projects or even develop their own project ideas. Group leaders will inform them
about upcoming Erasmus+ opportunities, solidarity projects or training courses. In this way, the exchange becomes a
starting point for longer-term involvement.

Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
From the very first day, we make learning visible and conscious. Participants are not only taking part in activities, but are
guided to understand what they are learning, why they are learning it and how it connects to their personal development.
On Day 1, we introduce the Youthpass process in a dedicated session. Instead of presenting it as a formal certificate at the
end, we explain the eight key competences and discuss what they mean in practice. Participants receive a simple reflection
sheet where the competences are described in accessible language. We clarify that Youthpass is based on self-evaluation
and that they are responsible for recognising their own learning. This sets the tone for the whole week.
Each day includes structured reflection time in “reflection families.” The method is consistent but varied enough to stay
engaging. Reflection usually happens in three steps:
1. Individual reflection (5 minutes): participants sit quietly, often with soft background music and answer guided questions in
writing. These questions connect directly to the day’s learning objectives (e.g. “What did I understand better about global
trade today?”, “Which skill did I practise during the debate?”).
2. Small group sharing (15 minutes): in stable reflection families, participants share insights, good and challenging moments
and give peer feedback.
3. Plenary highlights (10 minutes): key reflections are shared with the wider group, without pressure to speak.
This daily structure helps participants connect experiences with competences such as communication, teamwork, critical
thinking or civic engagement.
At the end of every workshop session, facilitators include a short debriefing. For example, after the Chocolate Simulation
Game, participants analyse not only the outcome but also what they experienced emotionally and cognitively. After debates
or dilemma cafés, we explicitly ask: “What did we practise here?” and “Why was this method chosen?” This makes the
learning objectives transparent and prevents activities from feeling random.
Learning is also documented through concrete outputs. Throughout the week, participants create flipcharts, posters, short
scripts, quiz questions and social media drafts. During the Campaign Design Labs, they prepare ready-to-use awareness
materials. In the Fair Trade Expo session, these final outcomes are presented in a marketplace format, giving participants a
clear overview of what they have produced together. This session functions as a collective summary of the thematic journey.
In addition, we will compile a simple digital booklet after the project. It will include photos (with consent), key learning points,
short participant reflections, workshop outlines and the final campaign materials. Participants are invited to contribute short
texts or quotes. This booklet will be shared with all partners and uploaded to relevant youth platforms such as SALTO, so
the learning becomes visible beyond the group itself.
During the final day, we dedicate extended time to evaluation and Youthpass. Participants first complete a personal self-
evaluation form, reflecting on how they developed in relation to each key competence. Afterwards, they discuss in small
groups what changes they noticed in themselves. Only after this process do we hold the Youthpass ceremony, so that the
certificate represents a real reflection, not just "attendance".
By combining daily reflection, clear debriefing after sessions, visible outputs and final documentation, we support
participants in becoming aware of their learning. The goal is not only that they understand fair trade better, but that they
recognise the competences they strengthened and can articulate them confidently in future studies, work or community
involvement.

Participant with fewer opportunities
Are participants involved in activities facing challenges that hinder their participation?
Yes
How will you reach out to these participants?
Yes, we intentionally involve young people who face obstacles that can limit their participation in international activities.
From each participating country, at least four participants will be young people with fewer opportunities. This is not a
symbolic target, but a conscious decision based on our everyday work with youth and the preparedness of our facilitators.
We reach out to these participants through multiple channels. First, partner organisations share the open call not only on
social media, but also directly through local youth centres, community groups and personal networks. Many of the young
people we aim to involve are not actively following Erasmus+ pages or NGO announcements. Therefore, group leaders and
volunteers actively approach young people they already know who might benefit from such an opportunity. This includes
young people living in rural or remote areas, those with limited financial means, young people with fewer educational
opportunities or those facing social exclusion.
Barriers linked to discrimination and cultural differences
Some participants may face discrimination based on ethnicity, social background, religion or identity. We create a safe and
inclusive selection process, focusing on motivation rather than formal achievements. During preparation, we clearly
communicate that the exchange is based on respect, equality and zero tolerance for discriminatory behaviour. By building a
diverse group and establishing ground rules together, we aim to reduce fear and hesitation connected to joining an
international group.
Barriers linked to education and training systems
Not all participants come from academic backgrounds. Some may be vocational students, early school leavers or young
people outside formal education. The application form is simple and accessible, avoiding complicated language. We do not
require previous Erasmus+ experience or high-level English skills. Basic communicative English is enough and during the
exchange, stronger speakers and group leaders support those who feel less confident. Non-formal methods such as role
play, group discussions and visual work allow everyone to participate regardless of academic background.
Economic barriers
Financial obstacles are one of the most common reasons why young people do not participate in mobility projects. Travel
costs are reimbursed according to Erasmus+ rules and if needed, sending organisations can purchase travel tickets in
advance so participants do not have to pre-finance their journey. Participation in the exchange itself is free of charge. We
clearly communicate this to avoid the perception that international projects are only for those who can afford them.
Geographical barriers
Young people from rural or remote areas often lack access to international opportunities. Through regional youth networks
and direct outreach, partner organisations ensure that the call reaches beyond capital cities. Preparatory meetings can be
organised online to reduce travel burdens before the exchange.
Social barriers
Some participants may lack self-confidence or experience anxiety about travelling abroad. The preparatory phase includes
online meetings where participants can meet others in advance, ask questions and reduce uncertainty. Group leaders
maintain close contact with their national group, offering individual support if needed. During the exchange, reflection
families provide a smaller, safer space to express concerns.
Overall, our approach is proactive and supportive. We do not label participants as “disadvantaged,” but we consciously
remove practical and psychological barriers. By combining open calls, targeted outreach, financial support, accessible
communication and a supportive learning environment, we aim to ensure that young people who normally have fewer
chances to participate in international projects can do so in a meaningful and empowering way.

discrimination. Our responsibility is not only to include them, but to actively support them before, during and after the

We do not plan accompanying persons, but instead rely on reinforced mentorship and structured support from group

Before the project

Support starts already during selection. The application form is simple and accessible, avoiding complicated language.
Motivation is valued more than experience. We provide clear and transparent information about reimbursements and
practical arrangements to reduce uncertainty.
For participants facing economic barriers, sending organisations can purchase travel tickets directly if requested, so they do
not have to advance money. Participation is free of charge. We also explain clearly what is covered (accommodation, 3
times a day meals, coffee breaks programme) to avoid hidden costs.
For those living in rural or remote areas, preparatory meetings can be held online. If necessary, we help them plan transport
to departure cities. Clear travel guidance is provided step by step.
For all participants, including the ones with lower confidence or limited international experience, national group leaders
organise preparatory meetings to discuss fears, expectations and practical matters. These meetings help build trust before
travelling. Participants are also invited to an international online meeting so they can see faces and hear voices before
arrival.
Language barriers are addressed early. We require only basic communicative English. During preparation, participants are
encouraged to prepare key vocabulary related to the topic. Group leaders reassure them that mistakes are acceptable and
that support will be available.
We also ask in advance about dietary needs, medical conditions or mental health considerations. This information is treated
confidentially and used only to ensure proper arrangements.
During the project
Each national group has a group leader who acts as mentor and first contact person. Group leaders monitor well-being daily
and provide individual support if needed. The hosting team holds coordination meetings with leaders to identify any
emerging difficulties.
Activities are designed using non-formal methods that allow different ways of participation. Not all sessions rely on
advanced language skills; many use visual elements, movement, small-group discussion and creative expression. This
ensures inclusion of participants with different educational backgrounds.
Reflection families provide a smaller and safer environment for sharing experiences. Participants who feel uncomfortable
speaking in plenary can express themselves in smaller groups.
Clear ground rules are co-created on the first day to prevent discrimination and ensure mutual respect. We actively promote
gender balance within groups and ensure equal speaking space during activities. If conflicts or misunderstandings arise,
facilitators intervene early and offer mediation.
Accommodation is shared in a way that respects comfort and gender considerations. Quiet spaces are available for
participants who need time alone. Health and hygiene rules are explained clearly. Participants are reminded to bring their
own necessary medication, as medical systems differ across countries.
After the project
Support does not stop after departure. Group leaders remain in contact with participants and encourage them to implement
follow-up activities at their own pace. Those who need additional guidance in organising local dissemination can receive
mentoring from their NGO.
Participants facing social or confidence-related barriers are especially encouraged to take small, manageable roles in
follow-up activities rather than feeling pressured to lead large events. Continued communication in the international group
also helps maintain connection and confidence.
Throughout all phases, the aim is to remove practical obstacles, reduce psychological stress and create a respectful,
inclusive environment. By combining financial support, clear communication, reinforced mentorship and inclusive
methodology, we ensure that young people facing barriers can participate meaningfully and safely, without the need for
accompanying persons.

Environmental friendly practices
Will you include sustainable and environmental-friendly practices in your activities?
Yes
Please describe them and mention how will you raise the awareness of participants on these sustainable practices?
Sustainability is not only the topic of our Youth Exchange, but also a principle guiding the way we implement it. As small
NGOs, we focus on realistic and consistent practices that reduce environmental impact and make participants more aware
of their daily choices.
First, travel is a major environmental factor. We encourage participants to use sustainable means of transport whenever
possible, especially train or bus instead of flights for shorter distances. Hungary is accessible by rail from several partner
countries and we actively inform participants about greener travel options during preparation. While we cannot fully control
travel choices, we raise awareness about the environmental impact of different transport methods and support planning in
advance to allow for more sustainable options.
The chosen venue in Eger supports our sustainability approach. It is accessible by public transport, reducing the need for
private cars. The programme is designed so that most activities take place within walking distance. For city-based activities,
such as the treasure hunt, participants explore the city on foot. We avoid unnecessary transportation during the week.
Regarding materials, we prioritise reusable and low-waste solutions. Instead of printing large amounts of paper, we use
digital tools (WhatsApp, Google Drive, Padlet, shared folders) for documents and preparation tasks. When flipcharts or
posters are used during workshops, they are reused for multiple sessions whenever possible. Participants are encouraged
to write on both sides of paper and avoid unnecessary printing. Name tags and decorative elements are made from recycled
or reused materials.
Single-use plastics are actively avoided. Participants are asked in advance to bring reusable water bottles. Drinking water is
provided in larger containers instead of small plastic bottles. Coffee breaks use reusable cups and dishes rather than
disposable items. We communicate this clearly in the infopack so participants can prepare accordingly.
Food is another important element. We request vegetarian or low-meat options as the default where possible, as this
reduces environmental impact. Dietary needs are respected, but we also use meals as a moment to discuss sustainable
food choices and their connection to global trade. Food waste is minimised by asking participants to take only what they can
consume and by coordinating closely with the accommodation regarding portions.
Waste separation is organised at the venue if facilities allow. Participants are informed about local recycling rules and
encouraged to separate waste properly. This is introduced as part of the opening days when ground rules are discussed.
Beyond practical measures, raising awareness is equally important. During sessions related to supply chains and the cost of
production, environmental impact is explicitly addressed: transport emissions, resource extraction, water usage and waste
in fast fashion. In reflection sessions, participants are invited to connect sustainability not only to trade fairness but also to
environmental responsibility.
The treasure hunt in Eger includes small tasks related to observing local sustainable practices, such as identifying eco-
friendly businesses or public recycling points. This links global topics to local reality.
In the Campaign Design Labs, participants are encouraged to consider environmental impact when planning their
awareness activities. For example, choosing digital dissemination over printed flyers or organising small events that do not
generate unnecessary waste.
Throughout the project, sustainability is presented not as a strict rule system, but as a shared responsibility. By combining
practical measures (transport, materials, food, waste reduction) with thematic discussion and reflection, participants
experience sustainability both in theory and in practice. This integrated approach makes environmental awareness part of
the daily experience of the Youth Exchange, rather than an abstract concept.

We chose our project partners based on previous cooperation, shared values and complementary competences in youth

youth empowerment and international cooperation, which makes the collaboration both practical and meaningful.
Street of Youth Association, as the hosting organisation in Hungary, has experience in organising local workshops and
Erasmus+ activities focused on sustainability, youth participation and non-formal education. Our approach is youth-led and
based on active involvement, which fits well with the structure of this exchange.
EurOpen Association from Romania brings experience in European citizenship education, ecological awareness and non-
formal youth activities. Their competence in working with young people from diverse social backgrounds strengthens the
inclusion dimension of the project. Active Youth Organisation from Estonia contributes experience in international youth
exchanges and civic engagement, particularly in empowering young people to take initiative in their communities. Colony of
Creators from Portugal has strong experience in creative methods, non-formal education and sustainability-related projects,
which supports the theatre, creative and campaign-design elements of the programme. ASWANA from Spain brings a

We formalise cooperation through bilateral partnership agreements signed after project approval. These agreements define
