Evaluation
How will you evaluate your project’s success? Which activities will you carry out in order to assess whether, and to what
extent, your project has reached its objectives and results?
We will evaluate the project’s success through a combination of continuous reflection, measurable feedback tools and
structured internal monitoring. We focus on methods that are realistic but meaningful, ensuring that evaluation is not just a
final formality but an integrated part of the whole process and clearly linked to our objectives.
At the very beginning of the exchange, participants will complete an anonymous baseline survey. This short questionnaire
will assess their initial understanding of Fair Trade, global supply chains, price distribution and consumer responsibility. It
will also ask them to self-assess certain competences such as critical thinking, teamwork, communication in English,
intercultural openness and confidence in expressing their opinions. The survey will include both scaled questions and open-
ended reflections. The same or very similar survey will be completed again at the end of the exchange. By comparing the
results, we can identify visible changes in knowledge, attitudes and self-perceived skills. This comparison gives us a
measurable indicator of development, even within a short-term mobility.
Evaluation is not limited to surveys. Daily reflection time in small “reflection families” is one of the core monitoring tools. Each
day participants first reflect individually in writing, then share in small groups and finally highlight key insights in plenary.
Reflection questions are connected to the learning objectives of the day and to the Youthpass key competences.
Participants identify what they understood better, which challenges they faced, how they solved them and what they would
improve. This ongoing self-evaluation supports awareness and allows facilitators to adjust methods if needed. If recurring
misunderstandings or tensions appear, they are addressed immediately rather than waiting until the final day.
Each workshop session ends with a short debriefing. Facilitators explicitly ask what the purpose of the activity was, what
participants experienced and which competences they practised. This helps us verify whether learning objectives were
achieved and prevents activities from being perceived as isolated exercises without meaning.
Midway through the exchange, we organise a structured mid-term evaluation. Participants can give honest feedback on
content, group dynamics, logistics and facilitation. Feedback can be given anonymously if preferred. The organising team,
facilitators and group leaders discuss this feedback and adapt the programme if necessary. This demonstrates that
evaluation is not symbolic, but influences the ongoing process.
The preparation of final outcomes is itself an important evaluation element. During the Campaign Design Labs and the Fair
Trade Expo, participants translate their knowledge into concrete materials such as workshop outlines, social media drafts,
quizzes or short presentations. The clarity and feasibility of these outputs show to what extent they understood the topic and
can apply it in practice. After the exchange, we will compile these materials, together with participant reflections and photos,
into a digital booklet. This booklet documents the learning journey and serves as tangible evidence of results.
After the mobility, participants complete the official Erasmus+ participant report. In addition, we plan a short follow-up
questionnaire one or two months later asking whether they implemented any local dissemination activities or whether their
consumption habits or engagement changed. This helps us assess medium-term impact beyond immediate satisfaction.
Partner organisations also take part in evaluation. We will organise a final online meeting where partners reflect on
cooperation, communication, inclusion measures and overall quality. Their perspective helps us assess organisational
success and identify areas for improvement in future projects.
Internally, we use a structured checklist and a simple quality book prepared during the planning phase. This document
tracks whether objectives were reached, deadlines respected, inclusion targets fulfilled and dissemination actions
implemented. The risk management document is reviewed to assess whether identified risks were handled effectively.
Financial tracking ensures compliance with Erasmus+ rules and responsible use of funds.
Success for us is measured through several indicators: visible improvement between pre- and post-survey results, active

dissemination activities and positive partner feedback. By combining quantitative surveys, qualitative reflection, practical

Sustainability of the results
What will you do to make sure that your project continues to have effects also after it ends?
We see this Youth Exchange not as a one-time event, but as a starting point for longer-term impact at personal,
organisational and community level. To ensure that the project continues to have effects after it ends, we focus on three
main areas: keeping the participants active, using the prepared outcomes in concrete dissemination actions and
strengthening long-term cooperation between partners.
First, we will keep the international group connected. The communication channels created before the exchange, such as
the common WhatsApp group and shared online folders, will remain active. Participants will be encouraged to share their
local dissemination activities, reflections and further ideas. We will post follow-up content regularly in the weeks after the
exchange, such as short video testimonials, quotes and photos. This keeps the experience alive and reminds participants of
their commitments. We will also inform them about further Erasmus+ opportunities and other youth initiatives, encouraging
continued involvement.
At national level, each group will be expected to implement at least one physical dissemination activity. This can be a small
workshop in a school, a presentation in a youth club or a discussion event in their community. The materials prepared during
the Campaign Design Labs, such as PowerPoint presentations, quizzes and workshop outlines, are designed to be directly
reusable. By using these tools, participants do not start from zero but build on what they created during the exchange. These
events will also include short information about Erasmus+ opportunities, encouraging other young people to participate in
similar projects.
Online dissemination will reinforce this impact. All partners will publish posts on their social media accounts, using shared
photos, videos and prepared texts. The hosting organisation will coordinate a small content package after the project,
including selected visuals, participant quotes and a short project summary. The final videos created during the exchange will
be shared by partners and uploaded on our social media accounts. Participants will also be invited to publish their own
reflections on their personal platforms.
A key sustainability element is the final digital handbook. This handbook will include a short summary of the project
background, learning objectives, descriptions of selected non-formal methods used and the ready-to-use campaign
materials. The base of this booklet will be the outcomes of the Campaign Lab Design workshop, created by the participants.
It will be available in English for free of charge. The handbook will be published on our website, on partners’ websites (if
applicable) and shared through youth platforms such as SALTO. This ensures that the results are accessible beyond the
immediate partnership and can inspire other youth organisations.
At organisational level, we will integrate the methods and experiences gained into our regular activities. The tested

expected to continue. During the final evaluation meeting, we will discuss potential follow-up ideas, such as a small-scale
partnership focusing on sustainable consumption or youth participation in local economic decision-making. Any future
initiative will be built realistically, based on capacity and shared interest.
At personal level, participants leave with increased awareness, practical skills and documented learning outcomes through
Youthpass. Youthpass not only recognises their development, but can also support them in education or employment
contexts. More importantly, the reflection process helps them see themselves as capable actors. This shift in confidence and
awareness can influence their everyday choices, discussions with peers and involvement in civic life.
The long-term effect of the project lies in multiplication. Each participant becomes a potential ambassador of responsible

Dissemination of project results
How will you make your project visible outside your organisation and partner organisations? How will you share its results
and success? With whom will you share the results?
We see visibility not as a formal obligation but as a natural extension of the project. From the beginning, participants are
encouraged to understand that what they learn and create should not stay inside one room in Eger. The project is designed
in a way that its results are shareable, understandable and usable for people outside our direct partnership.
Participants themselves play a central role in this. During and after the exchange, they are encouraged to share their
experiences on their own social media platforms. We will help them to create short ready-to-use texts, visuals and key
messages so they can post easily and accurately. Personal posts often reach beyond the usual NGO audience and make
the project visible to friends, classmates, colleagues and families. This peer-to-peer visibility is particularly important when
promoting Erasmus+ opportunities and topics like fair trade among young people who might not normally follow youth
organisations.
The most important visible outcome will be the final digital handbook. This handbook will not be a simple summary but a
practical tool created together with participants. It will include a short explanation of the project’s background and
objectives, a clear overview of the non-formal methods used (for example the Chocolate Simulation Game, Debate Corners,
Producer Voices Theatre Lab and Campaign Design Labs), and step-by-step descriptions so that other youth workers can
replicate them. It will also include the outcomes of the Campaign Design Lab, as ready-to-use materials such as workshop
outlines, quiz questions, social media campaign drafts, discussion questions and reflection methods. Participant reflections
and photos (with consent) will make the handbook more personal and authentic. The handbook will be published in English
and shared freely on our website and our partners’ websites, and uploaded to platforms such as SALTO to ensure
accessibility for other organisations across Europe. No registration or payment will be required.
In addition to the handbook, we will publish short videos and participant testimonials. These will be shared on our website
and social media platforms. Articles describing the learning process and results will be published as blog posts. Partners will
also share these materials on their own channels, multiplying reach.
We also plan a small social media mini-campaign after the exchange, based on the outputs of the Campaign Design Labs.
Participants will contribute content such as “Did you know?” posts about supply chains, short myth-busting statements
about Fair Trade and calls to action encouraging responsible consumption. This connects dissemination directly with
learning outcomes.
During the city-based activity in Eger, participants will engage with local people by asking simple, topic-related questions
such as “Do you know what Fair Trade means?”, “Would you pay more for a product if you knew the producer was paid
fairly?” or “Where do you usually buy your coffee?” These interactions raise awareness locally and encourage dialogue
beyond the project group. They also introduce Erasmus+ as the framework enabling such initiatives.
Youthpass is another visibility tool. Participants receive a certificate documenting their learning in relation to the eight key
competences. Many will use this in their CVs or educational contexts, indirectly promoting the value of Erasmus+ and non-
formal learning. When participants speak about their Youthpass experience, they demonstrate concrete results of the
project.
We will also share the results with local youth centres, schools and NGOs in each partner country. The prepared
PowerPoint presentations and workshop plans can be used in classrooms or community spaces. Inspired participants may
plan their own follow-up initiatives or even new Erasmus+ projects, further extending the impact.
Through participant-led dissemination, open-access materials, digital content, local outreach and European platforms, we
aim to make the project visible beyond our immediate network and ensure that its results are accessible and useful for a
wider audience.
How will you involve participants in such activities?
Participants will be involved in visibility and dissemination from the start, not only after the exchange ends. We treat sharing
results as part of the learning process, so participants don’t feel it is an extra “marketing task,” but something that belongs
to the project and strengthens its impact.
Before the mobility, we already prepare them for this role. In the preparatory meetings led by group leaders, we explain what
dissemination means in Erasmus+, why it matters and what is realistic for young people to do. We also ask participants
what they feel comfortable with: posting on social media, writing short texts, speaking in public, taking photos or helping
behind the scenes. This helps us involve everyone in a way that matches their confidence and skills. Consent is collected
early for photos and videos, and participants can choose whether they want to appear on content or only contribute
anonymously.
During the exchange, dissemination is built into the programme. Participants will not just “be photographed”; they will
actively create materials. For example, after certain workshops they will write short takeaways in their own words (one
sentence, one myth-buster, one surprising fact). These are collected daily and later turned into posts or handbook content.
In reflection families, they identify key moments and learning points that are worth sharing with others. This also helps them
recognise learning outcomes for Youthpass.
In the middle of the week, we dedicate short time within the programme to explain how to communicate the topic
responsibly. Participants discuss what makes a message clear, respectful and honest, especially when talking about global
inequalities. We encourage simple, factual communication rather than dramatic storytelling. This improves quality and
prevents misinformation.
For social media, we make it easy and structured. Participants will work in small international teams to create short content
